items.reality: Workflows are tradeoffs against release cadence, CI strength and team size. A startup deploying multiple times a day wants different mechanics than a library maintained for years across multiple supported versions.
items.example: Stripe (SaaS) ~ trunk-based. Django (versioned library) ~ release branches. Same tool, different policies.

items.reality: GitFlow's author himself walked back the recommendation; he now says it suits packaged software but is over-process for most web apps. Trunk-based + feature flags is the modern default for SaaS.
items.example: From the original 2010 post's 2020 update: 'these days, I find more often than not, GitFlow is the wrong choice.'

items.reality: Long-lived branches *increase* risk by delaying integration. Most production incidents come from the 'big merge' that combined two months of divergent work. Short branches + good CI is safer.
items.example: Conflict surface, untested-interaction surface and 'who owns this?' confusion all grow with branch lifetime.

items.myth: You can pick a workflow and never revisit it.
items.reality: Workflows should evolve with the team. A 5-person startup graduating to a 50-person org with mobile + web + library will likely need to add structure. Audit your workflow yearly.
items.example: Common arc: GitHub Flow → add environment branches at 20 engineers → add release branches when the library splits out.
